questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Which of the following is a vector quantity?
questions.hint: A vector has both magnitude and direction.
questions.explanation: Velocity has both magnitude (speed) and direction. Mass, time, and temperature are scalars with only magnitude. Option C is the only vector.

source_trace.content_anchor: vector is a physical quantity which can be expressed completely by stating both its magnitude with particular unit and direction

explanations_tiered.definition: A vector quantity has both magnitude and direction.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Vectors describe quantities like velocity and force, unlike scalars which have magnitude only.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Velocity tells you how fast AND which way; mass just tells how much.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Velocity is a vector because it includes direction; mass, time, temperature are scalars.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Vectors transform under coordinate rotations; scalars are invariant.
reasoning_steps: Check each quantity: does it have direction?
Mass, time, temperature have no direction.
misconceptions: Thinking speed is a vector (it is scalar; velocity is vector).
Assuming all measured quantities are vectors is incorrect.

glossary_entry.definition: Physical quantity requiring both magnitude and direction for complete description.

flashcard.front: Which is a vector: mass, time, velocity, temperature?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Which quantity is a vector: mass, time, velocity, temperature?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Quantity with both magnitude and direction.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Reinforce real-world examples of vectors vs scalars.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Velocity has direction; others do not.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: What are the components of the zero vector with initial and terminal points at the origin?
questions.hint: Zero vector has zero magnitude and no direction.
questions.explanation: The zero vector has both components zero: \((0,0)\). Options B, C, D have non-zero magnitude and represent non-zero vectors.

source_trace.content_anchor: if both initial and terminal points lie at origin, then v is zero vector: v = (0,0)

explanations_tiered.definition: The zero vector has zero magnitude and indeterminate direction, represented as \((0,0)\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: When initial and terminal points coincide, the displacement is zero in all directions.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you don't move at all, your displacement vector is (0,0).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Zero vector \(\mathbf{0} = (0,0)\) has no magnitude and no defined direction.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The zero vector is the additive identity in vector spaces: \(\mathbf{v} + \mathbf{0} = \mathbf{v}\).
reasoning_steps: If initial point = terminal point, displacement = 0.
misconceptions: Zero vector is not the same as scalar zero; it has components (0,0).
Some think zero vector has arbitrary direction; it is directionless.

glossary_entry.definition: Vector with zero magnitude and indeterminate direction, components (0,0).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the zero vector in component form?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Clarify that zero vector has no direction.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Zero vector components are (0,0).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: For vectors \(\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{w}\), the property \((\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v}) + \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{u} + (\mathbf{v} + \mathbf{w})\) is called:
questions.hint: This property involves grouping, not order.
questions.explanation: The associative property states that the grouping of addition does not affect the sum. Commutative involves order, not grouping. Option A is incorrect because commutativity is \(\mathbf{u}+\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{v}+\mathbf{u}\).

explanations_tiered.definition: The associative property states that the grouping of addition does not affect the sum.
explanations_tiered.beginner: It doesn't matter which two vectors you add first; the final sum is the same.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Associativity allows regrouping without changing result, analogous to scalar addition.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Associativity is a fundamental property of vector spaces: addition is a binary operation that satisfies \((a+b)+c = a+(b+c)\).
reasoning_steps: Add first two vectors, then third.
Add last two vectors, then first.
misconceptions: Confusing associative with commutative (order vs grouping).
Thinking associativity applies only to scalars; it applies to vectors too.

glossary_entry.definition: Property that grouping of addition does not change the sum.

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Associative property involves grouping.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: If \(\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0\) for non-zero vectors \(\mathbf{u}\) and \(\mathbf{v}\), then the vectors are:
questions.explanation: If dot product is zero and vectors are non-zero, then \(\cos\theta = 0\), so \(\theta = 90^\circ\) or \(270^\circ\), meaning perpendicular. Parallel vectors have dot product = \(\pm|\mathbf{u}||\mathbf{v}|\).

source_trace.content_anchor: If vectors are perpendicular or orthogonal, then u·v = 0

explanations_tiered.definition: Orthogonal vectors have a dot product of zero.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Dot product zero indicates the vectors are perpendicular (90° apart).
explanations_tiered.beginner: If the dot product is zero, the vectors are at right angles.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Orthogonality is a key concept in inner product spaces, generalizing perpendicularity.
misconceptions: Zero dot product could also mean one vector is zero, but condition says non-zero.
Some think dot product zero means vectors are parallel; that is incorrect.

glossary_entry.definition: Vectors with dot product zero, indicating 90° angle between them.

flashcard.front: What does \(\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{v}=0\) imply for non-zero vectors?

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Dot product zero implies perpendicular when vectors are non-zero.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Given \(\mathbf{u} = 2\mathbf{i} + 3\mathbf{j} + 4\mathbf{k}\) and \(\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{i} + 3\mathbf{j} + 5\mathbf{k}\), the magnitude of \(\mathbf{u} \times \mathbf{v}\) equals:
formula_used: \(\mathbf{u} \times \mathbf{v} = \begin{vmatrix} \mathbf{i} & \mathbf{j} & \mathbf{k} \\ u_1 & u_2 & u_3 \\ v_1 & v_2 & v_3 \end{vmatrix}\)

explanations_tiered.definition: The magnitude of the cross product equals the product of magnitudes times sine of the angle between them.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Cross product magnitude also gives area of parallelogram formed by the two vectors.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Cross product magnitude = \(|\mathbf{u}||\mathbf{v}|\sin\theta\), also area formula.
reasoning_steps: Compute i, j, k components carefully (j has negative sign).

formula_analysis.name: Cross product magnitude
formula_analysis.derivation: From determinant formula and Pythagorean theorem.

misconceptions: Forgetting negative sign on j-component leads to different magnitude.
Confusing cross product magnitude with dot product magnitude.

glossary_entry.definition: Length of cross product vector; area of parallelogram formed by the two vectors.

flashcard.front: Magnitude of cross product of (2,3,4) and (1,3,5)?

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 5.4 Application of Scalar and Cross Product
questions.subsection: 5.4 Application of Scalar and Cross Product
questions.question: Find the angle between \(\mathbf{u} = 4\mathbf{i} - \mathbf{j}\) and \(\mathbf{v} = 5\mathbf{i} + 3\mathbf{j}\).
questions.explanation: \(\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{v} = 4(5)+(-1)(3)=20-3=17\). \(|\mathbf{u}|=\sqrt{16+1}=\sqrt{17}\), \(|\mathbf{v}|=\sqrt{25+9}=\sqrt{34}\). \(\cos\theta = \frac{17}{\sqrt{17}\sqrt{34}}=\frac{17}{\sqrt{578}}=\frac{17}{17\sqrt{2}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\), so \(\theta=45^\circ\). Option A or C would have different cosine values.

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.4 Application of Scalar and Cross Product
source_trace.subsection_anchor: 5.4 Application of Scalar and Cross Product
source_trace.content_anchor: angle between u=4i-j and v=5i+3j is 45°

explanations_tiered.definition: The angle between vectors is found using dot product and magnitudes.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Find dot product, find each magnitude, divide, then inverse cosine.
explanations_tiered.advanced: This formula derives from the law of cosines: \(|\mathbf{u}-\mathbf{v}|^2 = |\mathbf{u}|^2+|\mathbf{v}|^2-2|\mathbf{u}||\mathbf{v}|\cos\theta\).
reasoning_steps: Divide dot product by product of magnitudes.
misconceptions: Using sine instead of cosine gives complementary angle.
Forgetting to take inverse cosine after computing ratio.

concept_map_nodes.target: Inverse cosine of dot product ratio

explanations_tiered.workbook_section: 5.4 Application of Scalar and Cross Product

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Find the vector equation of the line passing through \((2,2)\) with direction vector \((1,4)\).
questions.explanation: Given point \((2,2)\) as \(\mathbf{r}_0\) and direction \((1,4)\) as \(\mathbf{v}\), the equation is \((x,y) = (2,2) + t(1,4)\). Option A swaps direction components incorrectly.

explanations_tiered.definition: The vector equation of a line is \(\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{r}_0 + t\mathbf{v}\) where \(\mathbf{v}\) is direction.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: A point on the line plus any scalar multiple of the direction vector gives all points.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Start at (2,2), then add t times (1,4) to move along the line.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The parameter \(t \in \mathbb{R}\) generates all points; \(\mathbf{v}\) can be any non-zero scalar multiple.
misconceptions: Swapping point and direction vector (using direction as point, point as direction).
Using direction vector components in wrong order.

glossary_entry.definition: Equation using a point and direction vector: \(\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{r}_0 + t\mathbf{v}\).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Which statement is true about the cross product \(\mathbf{u} \times \mathbf{v}\)?

options.C: It always equals the dot product

questions.hint: Recall cross product order matters; swapping changes sign.
questions.explanation: Cross product is anti-commutative: \(\mathbf{u} \times \mathbf{v} = -\mathbf{v} \times \mathbf{u}\). Option A describes commutativity, which cross product does NOT have. Option D is wrong because cross product yields a vector.

explanations_tiered.definition: The cross product is anti-commutative: changing order changes sign.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Swapping the vectors gives the opposite direction (negative).
explanations_tiered.advanced: This follows from determinant properties: swapping rows changes sign.
reasoning_steps: Cross product uses determinant; swapping rows changes sign.
misconceptions: Assuming cross product commutes like dot product is wrong.
Thinking cross product yields a scalar is wrong; it yields a vector.

glossary_entry.definition: Property where swapping operands changes sign: \(\mathbf{u} \times \mathbf{v} = -\mathbf{v} \times \mathbf{u}\).

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Cross product is anti-commutative: \(\mathbf{u} \times \mathbf{v} = -\mathbf{v} \times \mathbf{u}\).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 5.4 Application of Scalar and Cross Product
questions.subsection: 5.4 Application of Scalar and Cross Product
questions.question: A force of 40 N is applied perpendicular to a 0.25 m wrench. What is the torque magnitude?
questions.hint: Torque = force × lever arm when perpendicular.
questions.explanation: Torque \(\tau = |\mathbf{r} \times \mathbf{F}| = rF\sin\theta\). Here \(\theta = 90^\circ\), so \(\sin 90^\circ = 1\). Thus \(\tau = 0.25 \times 40 = 10\text{ N·m}\). Option B would be for \(0.5\text{ m}\) lever arm.

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.4 Application of Scalar and Cross Product
source_trace.subsection_anchor: 5.4 Application of Scalar and Cross Product

explanations_tiered.definition: Torque is the cross product of position vector and force: \(\tau = |\mathbf{r} \times \mathbf{F}|\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Maximum torque occurs when force is perpendicular to lever arm (θ=90°).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Torque = force × distance when pushing straight sideways.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Torque is a vector perpendicular to both \(\mathbf{r}\) and \(\mathbf{F}\); its magnitude is \(rF\sin\theta\).
reasoning_steps: Identify lever arm length \(r\) and force \(F\).
misconceptions: Using cosine instead of sine gives zero torque for perpendicular case.
Forgetting units (N·m) is common.

flashcard.front: Torque when 40 N perpendicular to 0.25 m wrench?

concept_map_nodes.target: Cross product of r and F

explanations_tiered.workbook_section: 5.4 Application of Scalar and Cross Product

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: A line has parametric equations \(x = 1 + t, y = 4 - 2t\). What is the direction vector?
questions.hint: Coefficients of \(t\) give the direction vector.
questions.explanation: From \(x = x_0 + tp, y = y_0 + tq\), direction vector = \((p,q) = (1,-2)\). Option A uses point coordinates, not direction.

source_trace.content_anchor: x = x0 + tp, y = y0 + tq, direction vector = (p,q)

explanations_tiered.definition: Direction vector components are the coefficients of the parameter \(t\) in parametric equations.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For \(x = x_0 + pt, y = y_0 + qt\), direction = \((p,q)\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Look at the numbers multiplying t; those form the direction vector.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The direction vector is \(\mathbf{v} = (p,q)\); the point is \((x_0,y_0)\).
reasoning_steps: Identify coefficient of t in x-equation: p.
Identify coefficient of t in y-equation: q.
misconceptions: Using the point coordinates (1,4) as direction is incorrect.
Direction vector can be scaled; (2,-4) also represents same line.
explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Direction = coefficients of t: (1,-2).
